question: What evidence supports preemptive TEVAR for uncomplicated acute or subacute type B aortic dissection?
source_access_type: Original publisher PDF held in private local source workspace
source_version_or_date: European Journal of Cardio-Thoracic Surgery. 2024;65(2):ezad426. Published February 26, 2024
relevant_sections: Nomenclature and Risk Stratification - Acute aortic dissections
Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
Recommendation Table 7. Acute aortic diseases: type B aortic dissection
Table 3. High-risk features in acute type B aortic dissection
Figure 13. Treatment strategy for type B aortic dissection

extracted_claims.claim_text: For complicated acute type B aortic dissection with suitable anatomy, TEVAR is recommended.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: In patients with complicated acute type B aortic dissection and suitable anatomy, TEVAR is recommended.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: First recommendation in Recommendation Table 7; Class I; Level B. Included only to prevent applying the stronger complicated-TBAD recommendation to patients without complications or high-risk features.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: For acute complicated type B aortic dissection with anatomy unsuitable for TEVAR, FET repair should be considered.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: In patients with acute complicated type B aortic dissection with unsuitable anatomy for TEVAR, FET repair should be considered.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Second recommendation in Recommendation Table 7; Class IIa; Level B. Complicated-TBAD context only.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: For acute type B aortic dissection with high-risk features, TEVAR should be considered in the subacute phase.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: In acute type B aortic dissection with high-risk features, TEVAR should be considered in the subacute phase.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Third recommendation in Recommendation Table 7; Class IIa; Level C. This is selective treatment based on high-risk features, not routine TEVAR for every acute TBAD.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: For acute type B aortic dissection without high-risk features, optimal medical therapy, close monitoring, and follow-up are recommended to detect emerging high-risk features.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: In patients with acute type B aortic dissection without high-risk features, optimal medical therapy, close monitoring and follow-up is recommended for emerging high-risk features.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Fourth recommendation in Recommendation Table 7; Class I; Level B. Exact wording preserved, including the printed singular verb 'is.' The formal recommendation uses 'without high-risk features,' not 'uncomplicated.'
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline defines acute, subacute, and chronic aortic dissection by time from onset.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Aortic dissection is further subclassified based on symptom/dissection onset: (i) acute (up to 14 days after dissection onset); (ii) subacute (15-90 days after dissection onset) and (iii) chronic (91 days after dissection onset and later).
extracted_claims.section: Nomenclature and Risk Stratification - Acute aortic dissections
extracted_claims.figure: Figure 2. Type classification of aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The guideline does not add the STS/AATS hyperacute category in this timing sentence. Recommendation C03 places selective high-risk TEVAR in the subacute phase.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: Table 3 lists morphologic and clinical high-risk features in acute type B aortic dissection.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Morphologic criteria: Primary entry >10 mm; Primary entry at the inner curvature; Primary entry located <20 mm in relation to the left subclavian artery; False lumen diameter >22 mm; Descending thoracic aortic diameter >40 mm; High systolic antegrade flow volume in the false lumen with significant diastolic retrograde flow assessed by MRI. Clinical criteria: Persistent pain; Uncontrollable HTA.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.table: Table 3. High-risk features in acute type B aortic dissection
extracted_claims.source_location_note: HTA is defined by the table as hypertension arterialis. These criteria support the selective high-risk recommendation and should not be automatically equated with differently worded lists in other guidelines.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; clinical application pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The supporting text states that high-risk patients may benefit from best medical therapy plus endovascular stent graft treatment in the subacute phase.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Therefore, patients with high-risk features may benefit from best medical therapy (BMT) together with endovascular stent graft treatment in the subacute phase.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Supporting narrative for Recommendation Table 7. The narrative uses BMT, whereas the formal no-high-risk recommendation uses optimal medical therapy.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for extraction; pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The narrative contrasts immediate endovascular treatment candidates with initial medical management and monitoring when rupture, malperfusion, and high-risk signs are absent.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Cases of acute TBAD and clinical or CT signs of rupture, immediate malperfusion or high-risk features are candidates for immediate endovascular treatment. In the absence of the previously mentioned signs, careful monitoring and initial medical management are justified. However, these symptoms represent a complex, dynamic disease requiring optimal medical therapy and close monitoring in order to detect early progression of the disease.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: The immediate-treatment narrative should be reconciled by specialist review with the formal recommendation that TEVAR for acute TBAD with high-risk features should be considered in the subacute phase.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; timing interpretation pending specialist review

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline's ADSORB summary distinguishes unchanged early mortality from improved aortic remodeling after TEVAR plus medical therapy.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: In a small RCT of 61 patients (ADSORB trial), early mortality did not differ between BMT and patients undergoing TEVAR in addition to BMT, but cross-over to TEVAR was necessary due to the progression of the disease. Patients undergoing TEVAR had better outcomes in terms of aortic remodelling.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: Guideline summary of ADSORB. The remodeling finding must not be treated as proof of a mortality benefit; the underlying trial remains the primary source for outcome details.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for guideline-text extraction; underlying trial remains the primary source for numerical outcomes

extracted_claims.claim_text: The guideline reports differing clinical-outcome signals between a subacute cohort and INSTEAD-XL.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Recent data on a larger cohort of patients with TBAD in the subacute phase revealed lower 30-day mortality in patients undergoing early TEVAR in contrast to optimal therapy alone but no difference in 5-year outcomes. In the INSTEAD-XL trial, TEVAR in addition to BMT showed improved 5-year aorta-specific survival and delayed disease progression.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: These are guideline summaries of separate sources and must not be merged into a single effect estimate or interpreted as equivalence. Original publications remain authoritative for populations and methods.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for guideline-text extraction; underlying studies remain the primary sources for numerical outcomes

extracted_claims.claim_text: The narrative says TEVAR might be considered in selected patients without high-risk features when anatomy is suitable.
extracted_claims.exact_supporting_text: Based on the promising data on TEVAR in type B dissection without high-risk features and the better risk assessment by respecting high-risk features, TEVAR might be considered in those patients with suitable anatomy.
extracted_claims.section: Acute aortic diseases - Type B aortic dissections
extracted_claims.source_location_note: This narrative statement does not have a separate printed Class or Level and should be reconciled with the formal Class I recommendation for OMT, close monitoring, and follow-up when high-risk features are absent.
extracted_claims.confidence: High for transcription; scope and application pending specialist review

guideline_recommendations.population: Complicated acute type B aortic dissection with suitable anatomy

guideline_recommendations.recommendation: In acute type B aortic dissection with high-risk features, TEVAR should be considered in the subacute phase.
guideline_recommendations.population: Acute type B aortic dissection with high-risk features
guideline_recommendations.location: Printed p. 27; PDF p. 28; Recommendation Table 7; Table 3

guideline_recommendations.recommendation: In patients with acute type B aortic dissection without high-risk features, optimal medical therapy, close monitoring and follow-up is recommended for emerging high-risk features.
guideline_recommendations.population: Acute type B aortic dissection without high-risk features
guideline_recommendations.location: Printed p. 27; PDF p. 28; Recommendation Table 7; Table 3

limitations: Recommendation Table 7 uses the terms 'with high-risk features' and 'without high-risk features' rather than labeling the latter group 'uncomplicated.' These terms should not be forced into equivalence with the ACC/AHA phrase 'uncomplicated acute type B aortic dissection with high-risk anatomic features.'

STS/AATS explicitly categorizes high-risk TBAD separately from uncomplicated TBAD. EACTS/STS similarly stratifies the formal recommendations by presence or absence of high-risk features, but the criteria and recommendation wording should still be reviewed independently rather than assumed identical.
EACTS/STS defines acute as up to 14 days, subacute as 15-90 days, and chronic as 91 days or later. Unlike STS/AATS, this timing statement does not include a separate hyperacute category.

The formal high-risk recommendation specifies TEVAR in the subacute phase, while nearby narrative text describes acute TBAD with high-risk features as candidates for immediate endovascular treatment. Specialist interpretation is required.

The formal no-high-risk recommendation gives OMT, close monitoring, and follow-up a Class I recommendation, while nearby narrative text states that TEVAR might be considered in selected patients without high-risk features and suitable anatomy without assigning a separate Class or Level.

The narrative alternates between optimal medical therapy and best medical therapy. These source terms are preserved and not assumed to have different or identical protocol content without specialist review.
The guideline summarizes ADSORB, INSTEAD-XL, and observational data, but the original studies remain authoritative for population definitions, numerical outcomes, and risk-of-bias assessment.
Improved aortic remodeling is not proof of mortality benefit, and a reported lack of difference must not be interpreted as equivalence.
reviewer_notes: Original-source extraction only. All decisions remain Pending; no item is verified by a reviewer or suitable for generated answers. Complicated-TBAD recommendations are included only to prevent misapplication. No numerical outcome records were created from the guideline's secondary summaries of individual studies. Terminology differences from ACC/AHA and STS/AATS are documented without forcing equivalence.
